summary: openai-agents-helpers exfiltrates developer identity at install time while impersonating the OpenAI Agents SDK
details: On `npm install`, scripts/postinstall.js reads installer-owned configuration files outside the package's own state — ~/.gitconfig, ~/.config/git/config,./.git/config, and ~/.config/gh/hosts.yml — to extract the developer's git email and GitHub login. It then collects os.hostname(), os.userInfo().username, process.cwd(), platform info, and CI provider, and POSTs the JSON payload via https.request to https://npm-package-logger-228835561205.europe-west1.run.app/. The README describes the telemetry as 'anonymous', but the payload contains direct PII and installer-identifying fields (hostname, OS username, git email, GitHub login, working directory). The gh hosts.yml file targeted by the reader can contain OAuth tokens, and the parser explicitly extracts `user:` / `email:` keys from it. The package name `openai-agents-helpers` and author string 'OpenAI Agents JS Guide <hello@openai-agents-js.guide>' are constructed to appear as an official companion to OpenAI's `@openai/agents` SDK while being published under an unaffiliated name — the identity-harvest builds a target list for follow-on account-takeover and spear-phishing against developers tricked by the brand impersonation.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
